steps.title: Pin down the two-part claim
steps.content: The student argues (1) the mere presence of anger may not hurt perceptions of the review and (2) a high degree of anger does worsen perceptions of the review. Supporting data must therefore show low-anger reviews holding up against the control while high-anger reviews drop below it.

steps.title: Look for the matching contrast
steps.content: We need a comparison anchored to the control (no anger) on helpfulness of the review — low-anger ratings should be at least as high as the control, while high-anger ratings should be clearly lower.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• "On average, participants' ratings of the helpfulness of the review were substantially higher than were participants' ratings of the reviewed product regardless of which type of review participants had seen.": compares helpfulness with product attitude across all conditions, which says nothing about how anger level changes perception of the review. ✗
  • "Compared with participants who saw the control review, participants who saw the low-anger review rated the review as slightly more helpful, whereas participants who saw the high-anger review rated the review as less helpful.": uses the control as the baseline and shows low anger producing no harm to the review's perceived helpfulness while high anger lowers it — exactly the student's split. ✓
  • "Participants who saw the low-anger review rated the review as slightly more helpful than participants who saw the control review did, but participants' attitude toward the reviewed product was slightly worse when participants saw the low-anger review than when they saw the no-anger review.": introduces a negative effect from the low-anger condition, which contradicts the student's claim that mere anger may not hurt perceptions. ✗
  • "Compared with participants who saw the low-anger review, participants who saw the high-anger review rated the review as less helpful and had a less positive attitude toward the reviewed product.": compares high anger only to low anger and skips the control, so it cannot show that low anger is harmless relative to a no-anger baseline. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
